sections.content:

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 36 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The LEFT action caused an animation in the bottom-right grid. The animation shows 4 white 3x3 squares at positions (49,29), (54,24), (54,34), (59,29) all flashing/animating. But `board_changed: False` - so the final board is the same. This is just an animation effect.
Let me look more carefully at what the animation tells us. The cells_changed_per_frame shows a pattern: `[0, 9, 0, 0, 0, 0, 9, 0, 0, 0, 0, 9, 0, 0, 0, 0, 9, 0, 0, 0, 0, 36]` - this means cells flash on and off at different frames.
The animation objects show 4 white squares at specific positions in the 3x3 grid of the bottom-right panel. This looks like the LEFT key is selecting/highlighting certain cells in that grid.
Let me check the bottom-right grid pattern more carefully. The grid has:
- Row 1: gray, white, gray
- Row 2: white, gray, white  
- Row 3: gray, white, gray
This is a checkerboard pattern. The animated white squares are at positions that match the white squares in the grid.
Let me try RIGHT to see what happens, and also explore what MOUSE does.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check the animation details from LEFT action
print("=== Animation from LEFT ===")
if hasattr(last_action_result, 'executed_actions') and last_action_result['executed_actions']:
    anim = last_action_result['executed_actions'][0]['animation']
    print("Objects animated:", anim['objects'])
    print("Cells changed per frame:", anim['cells_changed_per_frame'])
    print("Board changed:", last_action_result['board_changed'])

# Now let's check if the board actually changed by comparing frames
print("\n=== Comparing before/after ===")
if last_transition:
    before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before_seg['nodes']}
    after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after_seg['nodes']}
    print("Before nodes:", len(before_nodes), "After nodes:", len(after_nodes))
    # Check if any node properties changed
    for nid in after_nodes:
        if nid in before_nodes:
            bn = before_nodes[nid]
            an = after_nodes[nid]
            if bn['hash'] != an['hash'] or bn['pixels'] != an['pixels']:
                print(f"  Node {nid} changed: hash {bn['hash']}->{an['hash']}, pixels {bn['pixels']}->{an['pixels']}")
        else:
            print(f"  New node {nid}")
    for nid in before_nodes:
        if nid not in after_nodes:
            print(f"  Removed node {nid}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: So the LEFT action didn't actually change the board state - it just caused an animation. The board is identical before and after. This is likely a decorative/flash animation. Let me try other actions to see what actually changes the board state. Let me try MOUSE on different elements.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try MOUSE on the bottom-left grid (the one with purple dots)
# The grid is at bbox [50,11]-[59,20], let's click center of one purple dot
# Purple dot at id=13: bbox [51,15]-[52,16], center around row 51, col 15
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 51, 'col': 15}])
print("Result:", result)
print("Board changed:", result.get('board_changed'))
if result.get('board_changed'):
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  id={n['id']:2d}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']:3d}, bbox=[{min(rows)},{min(cols)}]-[{max(rows)},{max(cols)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
